summary: Gruber vents his frustration at The New York Times for persistently showing a dismissal-proof referral banner (which he calls a 'dickbar') to paying subscribers. Despite being a loyal subscriber since 2011 paying $25 every four weeks, the Times pesters him multiple times daily with a yellow banner encouraging him to refer friends at a cheaper rate than he pays. He discovers that new subscribers pay significantly less than he does as a longtime customer, and that there's no way to downgrade or stop the banner. He uses an extended restaurant analogy to illustrate how absurd this treatment of loyal customers would be in any other context.
key_points: The NYT shows a persistent referral dickbar to paying subscribers with no 'don't ask again' option, covering content they're paying to read
Longtime subscribers pay more ($25/4 weeks) than the rate they're being asked to promote to friends ($17/4 weeks after intro), with no apparent way to downgrade
Canceling a NYT subscription requires a phone call with a retention agent, making it deliberately difficult to leave
Gruber uses the restaurant analogy to show how no sane business would repeatedly pester its best customers with referral pitches during the service they're paying for
The post highlights a broader pattern of subscription services taking loyal customers for granted while offering better deals to new ones
money_quotes: At some point a month or two ago (or maybe six months ago, or six days ago — who the hell knows in COVID time), The New York Times started pestering me with a bottom-of-the-page dickbar just about every damn time I visit their website.
After paying over $300 a year for nearly a decade, and having read the Times on a near-daily basis for my entire adult life, I feel I qualify as a good customer.
And they repay me by deliberately annoying me several times a day, every day, when I attempt to read the product I'm paying them for.
This analogy only goes so far. No sane restaurant would ever do this.
The purpose of the dickbar is to encourage me to convince my friends and family to sign up for this same treatment — paying to be pestered.
That's over $100 extra per year that they're just taking from me as a longtime subscriber who — until they angered me with this incessant yellow dickbar — never looked twice at my subscription.
Any friends I might refer get to pay significantly less than I — the longtime loyal customer — do.
key_insight: Subscription services that pester loyal, full-price customers with referral promotions offering newcomers a better deal are insulting the very people who sustain their business.
